rules.name: dag-title-case-in-prose-edit
rules.evidence: Write **Dag** (title case) in all prose. Keep the all-caps or lowercase spelling only when reproducing a literal code token — never rewrite these, even inside fenced code blocks.

rules.name: dag-title-case-in-prose-write
rules.evidence: Write **Dag** (title case) in all prose. Keep the all-caps or lowercase spelling only when reproducing a literal code token — never rewrite these, even inside fenced code blocks.

rules.name: no-directed-acyclic-graph-spellout-edit
rules.evidence: Don't spell out **Directed Acyclic Graph** except for historical context.

rules.name: no-directed-acyclic-graph-spellout-write
rules.evidence: Don't spell out **Directed Acyclic Graph** except for historical context.

rules.name: no-direct-host-python-commands
rules.evidence: Never run pytest, python, or airflow commands directly on the host — always use `breeze`.

rules.name: ruff-format-after-python-edit
rules.evidence: Always format and check Python files with ruff immediately after writing or editing them: `uv run ruff format <file_path>` and `uv run ruff check --fix <file_path>`. Do this for every Python file you create or modify, before moving on to the next step.

rules.name: ruff-format-after-python-write
rules.evidence: Always format and check Python files with ruff immediately after writing or editing them: `uv run ruff format <file_path>` and `uv run ruff check --fix <file_path>`. Do this for every Python file you create or modify, before moving on to the next step.

rules.name: no-assert-in-production-code-edit
rules.evidence: No `assert` in production code.

rules.name: no-assert-in-production-code-write
rules.evidence: No `assert` in production code.

rules.name: time-monotonic-not-time-time-edit
rules.evidence: `time.monotonic()` for durations, not `time.time()`.

rules.name: time-monotonic-not-time-time-write
rules.evidence: `time.monotonic()` for durations, not `time.time()`.

rules.name: no-session-commit-with-session-param-edit
rules.evidence: In `airflow-core`, functions with a `session` parameter must not call `session.commit()`.

rules.name: no-session-commit-with-session-param-write
rules.evidence: In `airflow-core`, functions with a `session` parameter must not call `session.commit()`.

rules.name: session-keyword-only-param-edit
rules.evidence: Use keyword-only `session` parameters.

rules.name: session-keyword-only-param-write
rules.evidence: Use keyword-only `session` parameters.

rules.name: guard-kubernetes-type-only-import-edit
rules.evidence: Guard heavy type-only imports (e.g., `kubernetes.client`) with `TYPE_CHECKING` in multi-process code paths.

rules.name: guard-kubernetes-type-only-import-write
rules.evidence: Guard heavy type-only imports (e.g., `kubernetes.client`) with `TYPE_CHECKING` in multi-process code paths.

rules.name: no-new-raise-airflow-exception-edit
rules.evidence: Never add new direct `raise AirflowException(...)` usages — the community is actively reducing them, not adding more, and the `check-no-new-airflow-exceptions` prek hook enforces this across `airflow-core`, `airflow-ctl`, `task-sdk`, `providers`, and `shared`.

rules.name: no-new-raise-airflow-exception-write
rules.evidence: Never add new direct `raise AirflowException(...)` usages — the community is actively reducing them, not adding more, and the `check-no-new-airflow-exceptions` prek hook enforces this across `airflow-core`, `airflow-ctl`, `task-sdk`, `providers`, and `shared`.

rules.name: translate-domain-exceptions-to-httpexception-edit
rules.evidence: Translate domain-layer exceptions to `HTTPException` at FastAPI route boundaries. In `airflow-core/src/airflow/core_api/` route handlers, catch errors raised by domain code ... and re-raise as `HTTPException` with the right status ... Otherwise they propagate as `500 Internal Server Error`, leaking internals and misleading clients.

rules.name: translate-domain-exceptions-to-httpexception-write
rules.evidence: Translate domain-layer exceptions to `HTTPException` at FastAPI route boundaries. In `airflow-core/src/airflow/core_api/` route handlers, catch errors raised by domain code ... and re-raise as `HTTPException` with the right status ... Otherwise they propagate as `500 Internal Server Error`, leaking internals and misleading clients.

rules.name: batch-bulk-delete-update-in-scheduler-loop-edit
rules.evidence: Bulk `DELETE`/`UPDATE` in the scheduler loop or any synchronous interval task (e.g. `call_regular_interval` callbacks) must be batched with `LIMIT` and committed between batches — never issue a single unbounded bulk write against a user-driven table.

rules.name: batch-bulk-delete-update-in-scheduler-loop-write
rules.evidence: Bulk `DELETE`/`UPDATE` in the scheduler loop or any synchronous interval task (e.g. `call_regular_interval` callbacks) must be batched with `LIMIT` and committed between batches — never issue a single unbounded bulk write against a user-driven table.

rules.name: apache-license-header-new-files
rules.evidence: Apache License header on all new files (prek enforces this).

rules.name: selective-checks-doc-sync
rules.evidence: Keep selective-checks behaviour and its documentation in sync. ... update `dev/breeze/doc/ci/04_selective_checks.md` in the same PR ... and add/adjust tests in `dev/breeze/tests/test_selective_checks.py`.

rules.name: newsfragment-only-when-certain-user-facing
rules.evidence: Golden rule: never create a newsfragment unless you are certain the change is user-facing. ... Adding a spurious newsfragment for a non-user-facing change is a defect, not a safe default.

rules.name: no-newsfragment-providers-or-airflow-ctl
rules.evidence: **Do not add newsfragments for `providers/` or `airflow-ctl/`** — their release managers regenerate the changelog from `git log` and do not consume newsfragments.

rules.name: no-unittest-testcase-edit
rules.evidence: Use pytest patterns, not `unittest.TestCase`.

rules.name: no-unittest-testcase-write
rules.evidence: Use pytest patterns, not `unittest.TestCase`.

rules.name: use-spec-autospec-when-mocking-edit
rules.evidence: Use `spec`/`autospec` when mocking.

rules.name: use-spec-autospec-when-mocking-write
rules.evidence: Use `spec`/`autospec` when mocking.

rules.name: prefer-mock-patch-decorator-edit
rules.evidence: Prefer `@mock.patch` decorators over `with mock.patch(...)` context managers for patching.

rules.name: prefer-mock-patch-decorator-write
rules.evidence: Prefer `@mock.patch` decorators over `with mock.patch(...)` context managers for patching.

rules.name: use-time-machine-not-datetime-now-edit
rules.evidence: Use `time_machine` for time-dependent tests. Do not use `datetime.now()`

rules.name: use-time-machine-not-datetime-now-write
rules.evidence: Use `time_machine` for time-dependent tests. Do not use `datetime.now()`

rules.name: no-caplog-text-assertion-edit
rules.evidence: Do not assert on raw log text (`caplog.text` for example), these are legacy string matching APIs planned for removal.

rules.name: no-caplog-text-assertion-write
rules.evidence: Do not assert on raw log text (`caplog.text` for example), these are legacy string matching APIs planned for removal.

rules.name: no-conventional-commit-message
rules.evidence: **Bad:** `fix(cli): dags test failure` — Airflow does not use Conventional Commits (`feat:`, `fix:`, `chore:` …). ... A `commit-msg` prek hook (`check-no-conventional-commit-message`) rejects these, and CI checks every commit of the PR.

rules.name: no-conventional-commit-pr-title
rules.evidence: Use the **imperative mood** and a plain message — do **not** use Conventional Commits prefixes (`fix:`, `feat:`, `chore:`, `docs:`, `refactor:`, …). ... The same rule applies to PR titles.

rules.name: run-prek-install-before-commit
rules.evidence: **Always run `prek install` before committing any code.** It installs the `commit-msg` hook (in addition to `pre-commit`) so the Conventional Commits guard runs locally.

rules.name: no-co-authored-by-self
rules.evidence: NEVER add Co-Authored-By with yourself as co-author of the commit. Agents cannot be authors, humans can be, Agents are assistants.

rules.name: no-push-upstream-or-main
rules.evidence: Always push branches to `origin`. Never push directly to `upstream` (and never push directly to `main` on either remote).

rules.name: check-existing-pr-before-creating
rules.evidence: Before working on an issue, check for open PRs already addressing it ... prefer reviewing and building on an existing PR. Open a separate one only *if your approach is genuinely different*.

rules.name: rebase-before-push
rules.evidence: Before pushing, always rebase your branch onto the latest target branch (usually `main`) to avoid merge conflicts and ensure CI runs against up-to-date code: `git fetch upstream <target_branch>` / `git rebase upstream/<target_branch>`.

rules.name: open-pr-not-issue-when-fix-known
rules.evidence: If you already know how to fix the problem and you (or the user) are going to open the PR shortly, **do not file a GitHub issue first**. Go straight to the PR.

rules.name: attribution-footer-on-github-posts
rules.evidence: Anything an agent drafts that ends up posted to GitHub on the user's account — PR / issue comments, PR-level reviews, line-level review comments, discussion replies — must end with an attribution footer.

rules.name: no-tag-individuals-in-github-posts
rules.evidence: AI agents MUST NOT mention or tag individual contributors, committers, PMC members, or maintainers using GitHub usernames (e.g. `@user`) unless explicitly instructed by a human reviewer.

rules.name: never-hand-edit-magpie-snapshot
rules.evidence: Adopter-specific modifications to framework-skill workflows live in `.apache-magpie-overrides/` — never edit the snapshot directly.

rules.name: no-secrets-in-commit
rules.evidence: **Never** ... Commit secrets, credentials, or tokens.

rules.name: no-hand-edit-generated-uv-lock
rules.evidence: **Never** ... Edit generated files by hand when a generation workflow exists.

rules.name: no-destructive-git-ops
rules.evidence: **Never** ... Use destructive git operations unless explicitly requested.

skill_candidates.summary: One-time development environment setup: install prek, enable commit hooks, install the per-machine breeze shim
skill_candidates.evidence: Install prek: `uv tool install prek` / Enable commit hooks: `prek install` / Install breeze shim (one-time, per machine): `scripts/tools/setup_breeze` — installs `~/.local/bin/breeze` that runs breeze via `uvx` from the current git worktree's `dev/breeze`.

skill_candidates.summary: Verify git remote naming (upstream/origin) against convention before remote operations and propose exact rename commands if mismatched
skill_candidates.evidence: Before running any remote-based command, run `git remote -v` and verify the names match this convention. If they do not ... do not silently go along with the existing names. Surface the mismatch to the user and propose the exact rename commands to bring the checkout in line with the convention, then ask the user to confirm before running them.

skill_candidates.summary: Pre-push self-review checklist: review full diff, check against code-review instructions and coding standards, run fast and manual static checks, run relevant/selective tests, check for security issues
skill_candidates.evidence: Before pushing, perform a self-review of your changes ... 1. Review the full diff ... 2. Read `.github/instructions/code-review.instructions.md` and check your diff against every rule ... 4. Run regular (fast) static checks ... 5. Run manual (slower) checks ... 6. Run relevant individual tests ... 7. Find which tests to run for the changes with selective-checks ... 8. Check for security issues.

skill_candidates.summary: Workflow for capturing deferred work when a PR is a workaround/mitigation: open the tracking issue first, reference it by number in the PR body, link the full issue URL as a code comment at the workaround site, and describe the workaround and acceptance criteria in the issue
skill_candidates.evidence: 1. **Open the tracking issue first**, before finalising the PR body. 2. **Reference it in the PR body by number** ... 3. **Add a link to the tracking issue as a comment at the workaround itself** ... 4. **Do not** write vague forward-looking phrases like "will open a tracking issue" ... 5. The tracking issue should describe: what the workaround is, why it was chosen, the concrete follow-up work needed, and any acceptance criteria for removing the workaround.

always_on: Repository is a UV workspace monorepo (airflow-core, task-sdk, providers, airflow-ctl, chart, dev, scripts, shared) with the key paths described in the Repository Structure section.

Architecture boundaries: Scheduler never executes user code; Workers never access the metadata DB directly (must go through the Execution API with a per-task JWT); Dag File Processor and Triggerer are steered through the Execution API by software guards that don't protect against intentional bypass.

When flagging security concerns, distinguish actual vulnerabilities (violate the documented security model) from known limitations (tracked gaps) and deployment-hardening opportunities — don't report the latter two as new findings.

Shared libraries under `shared/` are small, separately-versioned Python distributions symlinked into consumers (airflow-core, task-sdk); their own tests live in the shared distribution, consumer tests live in the consuming distribution.

SQLite is the default test backend; use `--backend postgres`/`--backend mysql` for integration tests needing those databases; if Docker networking fails, run `docker network prune`; if `uv.lock` conflicts, delete and regenerate with `uv lock` rather than hand-resolving.
Comment sparingly: code says what, comments say why — add a comment only when the reasoning is non-obvious, never to restate the next line or repeat rationale across multiple sites.
Keep imports at top of file except for circular imports, lazy loading for worker isolation, and `TYPE_CHECKING` blocks.

Use `conf_vars` (from `tests_common.test_utils.config`) for Airflow config overrides in tests — as a decorator when the value is fixed, as a context manager when it varies via `@pytest.mark.parametrize`.

Target exactly 100% coverage of what a PR changes — every changed/added behavior needs a test that fails without the change; don't add tests for pre-existing logic or for standard-library/third-party functions.
Consolidate near-duplicate tests that only differ in input/expected value into a single `@pytest.mark.parametrize` test.
Mark tests that require database access with `@pytest.mark.db_test`.
Test location mirrors source layout (e.g. `airflow/cli/cli_parser.py` → `tests/cli/test_cli_parser.py`); fixtures live in `devel-common/src/tests_common/pytest_plugin.py`.
Name functions/methods with action verbs (`get_`, `extract_`, `find_`, `compute_`, `build_`, …); avoid noun-only names — predicates (`is_`, `has_`) are the exception.
Put any generated files (PR reviews, reports, scratch output) under `files/`, creating the directory if it doesn't exist.
Commit messages should focus on user impact, not implementation details; the body should explain why the change was made, not restate what the diff already shows.
When apache-magpie is installed locally, prefer the `magpie-pr-management-code-review` skill for PR review over an ad-hoc pass or a generic review command.
Before large cross-package refactors, adding new dependencies with broad impact, or destructive data/migration changes, ask first rather than proceeding unilaterally.
Place temporary scripts in `dev/` (mounted as `/opt/airflow/dev/` inside Breeze).
